title: Conjectures on the Quotient Ring by Diagonal Invariants
overall_summary: This paper introduces and studies the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n = Q[x_1,...,x_n,y_1,...,y_n]/I for the diagonal action of S_n, where I is generated by all positive-degree diagonal invariants. It formulates foundational Hilbert-series and Frobenius-series conjectures relating R_n to trees, parking functions, q-Lagrange inversion, q-Catalan numbers, and the permutation module on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}. The main proven tools are the identification of R_n with diagonal harmonics via the apolar form, GL_2/sl_2 symmetry of the bigrading, straightening relations giving top-degree bounds, an explicit description of bidegrees linear in one variable set, and q-Lagrange identities matching the conjectural one-variable specialization. For the original problem, the paper is most useful as the foundational source for diagonal coinvariants, for the parking-function and q-Lagrange combinatorics expected in Hilbert/Frobenius specializations, and for the use of harmonic differential operators and GL_m symmetry to isolate Schur components.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Core definitions 1.1-1.4
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let X = {x_1,...,x_n} and Y = {y_1,...,y_n}. The symmetric group S_n acts diagonally on Q[X,Y] by sigma(x_i)=x_{sigma(i)} and sigma(y_i)=y_{sigma(i)}. Let I be the ideal generated by all S_n-invariant polynomials in Q[X,Y] with zero constant term, and define the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n = Q[X,Y]/I. Since I is bihomogeneous, R_n has components (R_n)_{i,j}. The Hilbert series is H_n(t,q)=sum_{i,j} t^i q^j dim (R_n)_{i,j}. If phi sends an S_n-character to its Frobenius symmetric function, the Frobenius series is F_n(t,q)=sum_{i,j} t^i q^j phi((R_n)_{i,j}).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: These are definitions. The paper observes that diagonal invariance and bihomogeneity are preserved by taking homogeneous and bihomogeneous components, so the quotient inherits a bigrading and an S_n-action in each bidegree. Frobenius series record the full S_n-character refinement of the Hilbert series; applying the inner product with p_1^n recovers dimensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2.1, Weyl generators for diagonal invariants
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the diagonal action of S_n on Q[X,Y], the invariant ring Q[X,Y]^{S_n} is generated by the polarized power sums p_{r,s}(X,Y)=sum_{i=1}^n x_i^r y_i^s, for nonnegative integers r,s. Consequently the ideal I defining R_n is generated by the positive-degree p_{r,s}, equivalently by any homogeneous generating set for Q[X,Y]^{S_n} with constants omitted.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Weyl's theorem on multisymmetric invariants. Since the ideal I is generated by all positive-degree invariants, any algebra generating set of the invariant ring generates I as an ideal. The same observation is stated to hold for any finite group action: homogeneous generators of the invariant ring generate the ideal of positive-degree invariants, and conversely.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.2.2, uniqueness of the trivial representation in R_n
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The bidegree (0,0) component of R_n affords the trivial S_n-representation, and this is the only occurrence of the trivial representation in R_n. Equivalently, if (i,j) is not (0,0), then (R_n)_{i,j} has no S_n-invariant vectors.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The class of 1 spans (R_n)_{0,0} and is fixed by S_n. If a nonconstant bihomogeneous class in bidegree (i,j) with (i,j) not (0,0) were S_n-invariant, it would be represented by an invariant polynomial of positive total degree. By definition every such invariant lies in I, hence maps to zero in the quotient. Therefore no other invariant vectors survive.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 1.3.1, apolar form and diagonal harmonics
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define the apolar bilinear form on Q[X,Y] by <f,g> = f(partial_{x_1},...,partial_{x_n},partial_{y_1},...,partial_{y_n}) g evaluated at X=Y=0. If I is any homogeneous ideal, then H=I^perp is a homogeneous space closed under all partial derivatives, and H = {h : f(partial_X,partial_Y) h = 0 for every f in I}. Conversely, if H is a homogeneous space closed under partial derivatives, then H^perp is a homogeneous ideal and H=(H^perp)^perp. For the ideal I defining R_n, the diagonal harmonics H_n=I^perp are S_n-stable and have the same bidegree S_n-characters as R_n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The key identity is adjointness: <f, partial_x g> = <x f, g> for every variable x. If h is orthogonal to an ideal I, then <f, partial_x h>=<x f,h>=0 for all f in I, hence partial_x h is also orthogonal to I. The differential-equation characterization follows because f(partial)h has all derivatives vanishing at the origin when h is orthogonal to all multiples of f. Conversely, if H is derivative-stable, then for f in H^perp one has <x f,h>=<f,partial_x h>=0 for all h in H, so H^perp is an ideal. Homogeneity permits taking double orthogonal complements degree by degree. Since the apolar form is S_n-invariant and nondegenerate on each bidegree, H_n and R_n are isomorphic as bigraded S_n-modules.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 1.5, classical one-set coinvariant formulas
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the ordinary S_n-action on Q[X] and the quotient by the ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials, the harmonic/coinvariant space has Frobenius series F(q) satisfying F(q)/((1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n)) = sum_{lambda partition of n} s_lambda(1,q,q^2,...) s_lambda. Hence the multiplicity polynomial of the irreducible character indexed by lambda is (1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n) s_lambda(1,q,q^2,...) = q^{n(lambda)} [n]_q [n-1]_q ... [1]_q / product_{cells x in lambda} [h(x)]_q = sum_{T in SYT(lambda)} q^{maj(T)}. The Hilbert series is [n]_q! = [n]_q [n-1]_q ... [1]_q, and the ungraded S_n-module is the regular representation.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The fundamental symmetric functions form a homogeneous system of parameters in Q[X], and Q[X] is free over its invariant subring. The harmonic space maps isomorphically to the quotient and gives the free basis, so the Frobenius series of Q[X] factors as the product of the invariant-ring Hilbert series and the coinvariant Frobenius series. The displayed Schur-principal-specialization formula then gives the irreducible multiplicities; the q-hook and major-index forms are standard symmetric-function identities. The Hilbert series follows by summing dimensions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.2.1, one-variable edge specialization
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the diagonal coinvariant ring R_n in two variable sets, H_n(q,0)=H_n(0,q)=[n]_q!. The analogous Frobenius specialization F_n(q,0), or F_n(0,q), is the ordinary one-set coinvariant Frobenius series from Section 1.5.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Taking degree zero in Y leaves only polynomials in X. The generators of I in this subring are exactly the positive-degree symmetric polynomials in X, so the degree-zero-in-Y part of R_n is the ordinary coinvariant algebra. The same argument applies with X and Y interchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.2, polynomiality criterion for principal Schur quotients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix positive integers n and p. The following are equivalent: for every partition lambda of n, s_lambda(1,q,...,q^{p-1})/(1+q+...+q^{p-1}) is a polynomial; the same quotient is a polynomial for lambda=(n); and p is relatively prime to n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof replaces the Schur basis by the complete homogeneous basis h_lambda. One has h_r(1,q,...,q^{p-1}) = [p+r-1 choose r]_q = [p]_q [p+1]_q ... [p+r-1]_q / [r]_q!. The roots of [p]_q=1+q+...+q^{p-1} are the nontrivial p-th roots of unity. If such a root is primitive of order d dividing p, then it appears in h_r(1,q,...,q^{p-1}) with multiplicity 1 precisely when d does not divide r. Thus [p]_q divides h_lambda iff for every d>1 dividing p, at least one part of lambda is not divisible by d. This holds for all lambda partitioning n iff no d>1 divides both p and n, i.e. gcd(p,n)=1. The case lambda=(n) gives the same condition.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.3, Koszul interpretation of the principal Schur quotient
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Suppose Q[X]=Q[x_1,...,x_n] contains a homogeneous system of parameters theta_1,...,theta_n such that theta_1 has degree 1, theta_2,...,theta_n have degree p, theta_1 is S_n-invariant, and theta_2,...,theta_n span the irreducible reflection representation of S_n. Then the Frobenius series of Q[X]/(theta_1,...,theta_n) is sum_{lambda partition of n} s_lambda(1,q,...,q^{p-1})/(1+q+...+q^{p-1}) s_lambda.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Koszul resolution of Q[X]/(theta_1,...,theta_n). Each free module in the resolution has a graded S_n-basis indexed by subsets of the parameters, and the maps are homogeneous and S_n-equivariant. Exactness implies that the quotient Frobenius series is the alternating sum of the Frobenius series of the free terms. This alternating sum is the internal product of the Frobenius series of Q[X] with the character-valued exterior generating function of the parameter representation. The latter is computed from one invariant parameter of degree 1 and an (n-1)-dimensional reflection representation in degree p; simplifying the internal product gives the displayed Schur-principal-specialization quotient. Since it is a Frobenius series of an actual quotient, the coefficients are nonnegative integers.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.5.4, existence of the parameters when gcd(p,n)=1
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A homogeneous system of parameters satisfying Proposition 2.5.3 exists whenever p is relatively prime to n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Take theta_1=x_1+...+x_n. For p prime not dividing n, choose theta_i=x_i^p-x_{i-1}^p for 2≤i≤n. If theta_i=0 for all i and theta_1=0, then all x_i^p are equal. A nonzero solution may be rescaled so x_1=1 and every x_i is a p-th root of unity. Then sum_i x_i=0 gives an integer polynomial f with f(xi_p)=0 at a primitive p-th root xi_p and f(1)=n; since the minimal polynomial is 1+x+...+x^{p-1}, evaluating at 1 forces p to divide n, contradiction. For composite p relatively prime to n, the paper shows closure under multiplying degrees: after modding out theta_1, a suitable system corresponds to an S_n-equivariant homogeneous polynomial map from the reflection representation to itself whose only preimage of 0 is 0. Composing such maps for degrees p and q gives one of degree pq. Factoring p into primes not dividing n yields the result.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6.1, parking functions and Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A parking function is a function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n} such that for every 1≤k≤n, the set f^{-1}({1,...,k}) has cardinality at least k. The number of parking functions is (n+1)^{n-1}. Under the action of S_n by permuting the domain, the permutation representation on parking functions is isomorphic to the permutation representation on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}, where S_n permutes coordinates and the quotient is by the diagonal subgroup generated by (1,1,...,1).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Consider the parking process on a circular one-way street with n+1 spaces. For every preference function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n+1}, all cars park and exactly one space remains empty. Among the n+1 cyclic rotations of f, exactly one has the empty space equal to n+1, and that rotated preference function is a parking function in the usual n-space sense. Thus all functions to n+1 decompose into n+1 copies of the set of parking functions, equivariantly for the S_n-action on cars. Functions to n+1 form the permutation representation on Z_{n+1}^n, and quotienting by simultaneous cyclic rotation gives Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 2.6.2, parking-function weight and tree inversions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a parking function f:{1,...,n}->{1,...,n}, define its weight by w(f)=n(n+1)/2 - sum_i f(i). Then sum over parking functions f of q^{w(f)} equals the inversion enumerator sum_T q^{inv(T)} over spanning trees T on vertices {0,1,...,n}, rooted at 0, where an inversion is a pair i<j in {1,...,n} such that j is an ancestor of i.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper cites Kreweras for a bijective proof. It also later derives the identity via q-Lagrange inversion: after specializing elementary symmetric functions e_k to 1/k! in the explicit q-Lagrange solution h_n^*(q), the resulting exponential generating function agrees with Gessel's q-Lagrange formula for the Mallows-Riordan inversion enumerator for labelled trees.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.1.1, GL_2 and sl_2 decomposition
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The natural GL_2-action mixing the two rows X and Y commutes with S_n and preserves R_n and H_n. With sl_2 operators E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}, F=sum_i x_i partial_{y_i}, and H=sum_i y_i partial_{y_i} - x_i partial_{x_i}, each bidegree (H_n)_{i,j} has H-weight j-i. Consequently F_n(q^{-1},q) is a sum of irreducible SL_2 x S_n characters of the form (q^{-m}+q^{-m+2}+...+q^{m-2}+q^m) s_lambda. The same holds in each fixed total degree, so along each total-degree diagonal the coefficient of each S_n-irreducible is symmetric and unimodal; the same symmetric-unimodal conclusion holds for the Hilbert-series diagonals.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The GL_2-action is left multiplication on the two-row matrix of variables and commutes with column permutations by S_n. Since it sends invariant polynomials to invariant polynomials, it preserves I and hence R_n; via the apolar form it also preserves H_n. Finite-dimensional SL_2-modules decompose into irreducibles V_m with weights -m,-m+2,...,m. Because the S_n-action commutes with SL_2, H_n decomposes into sums V_m tensor W_lambda. Substituting t=q^{-1} records exactly the H-weight, giving the displayed character form and unimodality.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 3.2.1, straightening relations from complete symmetric functions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For 1≤k≤n and r+s=k, the polynomial obtained as the coefficient of alpha^r beta^s in h_k(alpha x_k+beta y_k, ..., alpha x_n+beta y_n) belongs to I, where h_k is the complete homogeneous symmetric function of degree k.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices first to prove h_k(x_k,...,x_n) in I. Since product_{i=1}^n (1+t x_i)=sum_j e_j(X)t^j is congruent to 1 modulo I, one has product_{i=1}^{k-1}(1+t x_i) congruent to product_{i=k}^n (1+t x_i)^{-1}=sum_j (-t)^j h_j(x_k,...,x_n) modulo I. The coefficient of t^k on the left is zero because the left product has degree at most k-1, so h_k(x_k,...,x_n) is in I. The stated alpha,beta-polarizations are obtained from h_k(x_k,...,x_n) by applying the operator E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i} repeatedly, and I is stable under this GL_2 action.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.2.2, monomial spanning and top total degree bound
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The quotient R_n is spanned by monomials m(X,Y) such that for each k, the total degree of m in x_k and y_k is strictly less than k. In particular, (R_n)_{i,j}=0 whenever i+j>binom(n,2).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Order monomials lexicographically by total degree in the pairs (x_1,y_1), then (x_2,y_2), and so on, with any tie-breaking. In Lemma 3.2.1 the leading term of the relation with r+s=k is x_k^r y_k^s. Thus any monomial containing a factor x_k^r y_k^s of total degree k can be reduced modulo I to a linear combination of lexicographically smaller monomials. Iterating terminates and leaves only monomials with pair-degree less than k for every k. The maximum total degree of such monomials is sum_{k=1}^n (k-1)=binom(n,2).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 3.2.3, tail-degree vanishing criterion for monomials
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If a monomial m(X,Y) has total degree in the variables x_k,y_k,...,x_n,y_n exceeding (k-1)+k+...+(n-1), then m(X,Y) is congruent to 0 modulo I.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Such a monomial must be subject to one of the straightening relations of Corollary 3.2.2 within the tail variables. Applying a straightening step preserves the property that the tail degree is too large, while lowering the chosen monomial order. Iteration must terminate; no admissible monomial can have tail degree exceeding the stated maximum, so the result is zero in the quotient.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.2.4, one-dimensional top total degree
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For every bidegree (i,j) with i+j=binom(n,2), the component (R_n)_{i,j} is one-dimensional.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: By Corollary 3.2.2, any surviving monomial in total degree binom(n,2) must have pair-degree exactly k-1 in x_k,y_k for every k. Thus monomials have the form product_k x_k^{r_k} y_k^{s_k} with r_k+s_k=k-1. The proof compares two adjacent pairs k,k+1. Applying the transposition (k,k+1) to a monomial and straightening via Lemma 3.2.1 gives a relation expressing the transposed monomial in terms of two nearby exponent redistributions. Applying the same relation twice yields a tridiagonal linear relation among monomials m_a indexed by the number of x_k's assigned to the kth pair while keeping the total x-degree across pairs k,k+1 fixed. The tridiagonal matrix has positive entries on its three diagonals and each column sums to 1, so by elementary Markov-chain theory its eigenvalue-1 eigenspace is one-dimensional, forcing all m_a to be congruent. Repeating this adjacent redistribution moves all x-exponents before all y-exponents and reduces every admissible monomial of bidegree (i,j) to the unique ordered monomial of that bidegree.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 3.3.1, diagonal harmonics linear in Y
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let (H_n)_{d,1} be the component of diagonal harmonics of X-degree d and Y-degree 1. Then sum_d t^d phi((H_n)_{d,1}) = (1+t+...+t^{n-2}) sum_{lambda partition of n} t^{binom(n-1,2)} K_{lambda,mu}(t^{-1}) s_lambda, where mu=(2,1^{n-2}) and K_{lambda,mu}(t) are the Hall-Littlewood t-Kostka coefficients.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof, due to Alfano and Wallach, uses harmonic-preserving operators. Besides E=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}, use D=sum_i y_i partial_{y_i} partial_{x_i}. Starting from the Vandermonde Delta(X), the polynomials D^k E Delta(X), for 0≤k≤n-2, and all their X-derivatives are diagonal harmonic and linear in Y. The top element D^{n-2}E Delta(X) is, up to scalar, sum_i (-1)^i y_i Delta(x_1,...,x_{i-1},x_{i+1},...,x_n). A result of Garsia-Procesi and Bergeron-Garsia gives the Frobenius series of its X-derivative span as sum_lambda t^{binom(n-1,2)} K_{lambda,mu}(t^{-1}) s_lambda. Let A_k be the derivative span of D^k E Delta(X). The operator D induces S_n-equivariant degree-lowering surjections A_k/(A_k cap A_{k+1}) -> A_{k+1}/(A_{k+1} cap A_{k+2}), so the sum A=sum_k A_k has Frobenius series at least the displayed product, with equality if dim A≤(n-1)n!/2. To get the bound, place A inside the space T of all sums_i y_i f_i with f_i ordinary X-harmonic and sum_i f_i=0, which has dimension (n-1)n!. Compare the apolar orthogonal complement B of A in T with the diagonal-harmonic equations: writing f_i=g_i(partial_X)Delta(X), the equations defining B and those defining (H_n)_{-,1} are dual copies of the same family sum_i partial_{x_i}^k g_i(partial_X)Delta(X)=0. This gives dim B=dim(H_n)_{-,1} and B+(H_n)_{-,1}=T, forcing dim A≤(n-1)n!/2 and hence equality throughout.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.4.1, explicit solution of the Andrews-Gessel-Garsia q-Lagrange coefficients
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let H(z)=sum_{k≥0} h_k z^k with h_0=1, and define e_k by 1/H(-z)=sum_{k≥0} e_k z^k. Let h_n^*(q) be the symmetric functions determined by the q-Lagrange inverse relation z = sum_{k≥0} q^{-binom(k,2)} h_k^*(q) z^{k+1} H(-z) H(-q^{-1}z) ... H(-q^{-k}z). Then h_n^*(q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}, where delta_n=(n-1,n-2,...,1,0), the sum is over partitions mu contained in delta_n, and lambda(mu) is the partition of n whose parts are the multiplicities m_i(mu) of the parts i of mu, including m_0 so that the total number of parts is n.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof rewrites the q-inverse relation using q-functional composition. Dividing the defining equation by zH(-z), replacing z by qz, and applying the basic inverse identity for q-composition yields eH^*(z;q) = (1/H(-qz)) composed_q with (z eH^*(z;q)), where eH^*(z;q)=sum q^k h_k^*(q) z^k. Comparing coefficients gives the recurrence h_n^*(q)=sum_{k=1}^n q^{binom(k,2)} e_k sum_{n_1+...+n_k=n-k} product_{i=1}^k q^{(i-1)n_i} h_{n_i}^*(q). To verify the closed formula, decompose each partition mu contained in delta_n by k, the number of zero parts. For 1≤i≤k, let p_i be the largest index whose row of mu does not fit in delta_{n-i}, and set p_i=n_1+...+n_i. The rows between p_{i-1} and p_i contribute the factor q^{(i-1)n_i} h_{n_i}^*(q), while the k zero parts contribute q^{binom(k,2)} e_k. This exactly matches the recurrence.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 4.5.1, Frobenius series of modules in a conjectural exact sequence
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n and 0≤k≤n. Let R_{n-k} be graded by complement of X-degree, so its Frobenius series is denoted F_{n-k}(q). Tensor this S_{n-k}-module with the S_k-module Q[x_1,...,x_k]/(x_1^{n+1-k},...,x_k^{n+1-k}), graded in the usual way, and induce the resulting S_k x S_{n-k}-module to S_n. If H(z)=sum_{r≥0} h_r z^r is the complete symmetric-function generating series, the Frobenius series of this induced module is F_{n-k}(q) times the coefficient of z^k in H(z)H(qz)...H(q^{n-k}z).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It remains only to compute the Frobenius series of Q[x_1,...,x_k]/(x_i^{n+1-k}). The sequence x_1^{n+1-k},...,x_k^{n+1-k} is a homogeneous system of parameters, so the Koszul resolution gives the quotient character as the internal product of the polynomial-ring Frobenius series with the exterior alternating contribution of the parameters. The calculation simplifies to the coefficient of z^k in H(z)H(qz)...H(q^{n-k}z). Induction from S_k x S_{n-k} to S_n corresponds to multiplication of Frobenius characteristics, giving the displayed product with F_{n-k}(q).

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 4.5.2, exact sequence implication for the parking-function Frobenius conjecture
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If for each n there exists an exact sequence 0 -> R_n^{(n)} -> R_n^{(n-1)} -> ... -> R_n^{(0)}=R_n -> 0 of graded S_n-modules, where R_n^{(k)} is the module described in Proposition 4.5.1, then the conjectural formula F_n(1,q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)} holds.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The q-Lagrange identity in Proposition 4.4.1, after rewriting with F_n(q)=q^{binom(n,2)}F_n(1,q^{-1}), gives an alternating sum identity among the Frobenius series appearing in Proposition 4.5.1. An exact sequence with those terms would imply the alternating sum of Frobenius characteristics is zero. Solving the resulting recurrence is exactly the q-Lagrange solution, giving the stated formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.2.1, enlarging the operator algebra does not enlarge the generated harmonic space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Delta(X)=product_{i<j}(x_i-x_j). Define E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p for p>0. Consider also all S_n-invariant differential operators linear in the variables x_i,y_i and homogeneous of nonpositive total degree, of the form sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k f(partials in all variables except x_i,y_i), and the analogous operators with x and y interchanged, where f is diagonally S_{n-1}-invariant. The smallest space containing Delta(X) and closed under all partial derivatives and all such operators is the same as the space generated from Delta(X) using only the E_p and the partial derivatives partial_{x_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The paper first notes that the large operator algebra is generated by pure invariant differential operators together with E^0_{h,k}=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k and E^1_{h,k}=sum_i x_i partial_{x_i}^h partial_{y_i}^k. Since pure invariant negative-degree differential operators kill harmonics, only products of the E^a_{h,k} and ordinary derivatives matter. Their span is a Lie algebra, and the Poincare-Birkhoff-Witt theorem lets one order products so that all factors with k>0, h>n-1, or of type E^1_{h,0} act last. Such factors either kill Delta(X) or act by scalar/eigenvalue behavior after the preceding reductions. Therefore only the operators E^0_{p,0}=E_p with 1≤p≤n-1 and the X-derivatives are needed.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollary 5.2.2, symmetry of X- and Y-generated operator spaces
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The space generated from Delta(X) by the operators E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and the derivatives partial_{x_i} is equal to the space generated from Delta(Y) by the operators F_p=sum_i x_i partial_{y_i}^p and the derivatives partial_{y_i}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The first generated space contains Delta(Y), because repeated application of E_1 to Delta(X) produces Delta(Y) up to a scalar. By Proposition 5.2.1 this first space is closed under the larger symmetric operator algebra, including the Y-version operators F_p and Y-derivatives, so it contains the second generated space. The reverse inclusion follows by interchanging X and Y.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.3.1, substitution criterion for the orthogonal ideal of the operator-generated space
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the ideal orthogonal, under the apolar form, to the space generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and partial_{x_i}. Then J consists exactly of those f(X,Y) such that f(x_1,...,x_n, phi_a(x_1),...,phi_a(x_n)) lies in the classical one-set coinvariant ideal generated by positive-degree symmetric polynomials in X, for every polynomial phi_a(x)=a_1 x+a_2 x^2+...+a_{n-1}x^{n-1}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The adjoint of E_p is E_p^*=sum_i x_i^p partial_{y_i}. Thus f is in J iff for every sequence of exponents, (E_1^*)^{e_1}...(E_{n-1}^*)^{e_{n-1}} f, evaluated at Y=0, is orthogonal to all X-derivatives of Delta(X), equivalently belongs to the ordinary coinvariant ideal in Q[X]. The exponential exp(a_1E_1^*+...+a_{n-1}E_{n-1}^*) translates each y_i to y_i+phi_a(x_i). Setting Y=0 gives exactly the family of substitutions in the statement, and taking all coefficients in the a_i is equivalent to requiring all iterated adjoint conditions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.5.2, operator rings as subrings of an ordinary coinvariant quotient
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let J be the ideal in Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}] consisting of all polynomials p such that the differential operator p(partial_{x_1},...,partial_{x_n},E_1,...,E_{n-1}) kills Delta(X), and define T_n=Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/J and U_n=Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(J cap Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]). Let N=Q[X,Y]/(p_1(X),...,p_n(X)), where p_i(X) are fundamental symmetric power sums in X. Then T_n is isomorphic to the subring of N generated by the x_i and by e_k=sum_i y_i x_i^k, and U_n is isomorphic to the subring generated by the e_k alone.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: A differential operator polynomial in y_i and partial_{x_i} kills Delta(X) precisely when each coefficient, after replacing partial_{x_i} by x_i, lies in the classical ideal generated by the symmetric functions p_1(X),...,p_n(X). Under this replacement, the operator E_k=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^k corresponds to the polynomial sum_i y_i x_i^k. Therefore the quotient by operators annihilating Delta(X) is exactly the indicated subring of N.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 5.5.3 and 5.5.4, complete-intersection models for the q=t^{-1} predictions
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: If eU_n=Q[e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(theta_1,...,theta_{n-1}) with deg(e_k)=k+1 and theta_i a homogeneous system of parameters of degrees n+i+1, then the Hilbert series of eU_n is the conjectural Hilbert series for U_n, namely (1-q^{n+2})...(1-q^{2n})/((1-q^2)...(1-q^n)). If eT_n=Q[x_1,...,x_n,e_1,...,e_{n-1}]/(theta_1,...,theta_{2n-1}), with deg(x_i)=1, deg(e_k)=k+1, theta_1,...,theta_n S_n-invariants of degrees 1,...,n, and theta_{n+1},...,theta_{2n-1} of degree n+1 spanning the irreducible reflection representation, then the Frobenius series of eT_n is the conjectural Frobenius series for T_n, obtained by applying the involution omega to the specialization in Conjecture 2.5.1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For eU_n the formula is the standard Hilbert series of a complete intersection: multiply the numerator factors 1-q^{deg theta_i} and divide by the variable-degree factors 1-q^{deg e_k}. For eT_n, the invariant parameters theta_1,...,theta_n cancel the ordinary polynomial-ring invariant contributions, while the degree n+1 reflection-representation parameters are handled by the Koszul calculation of Proposition 2.5.3. The remaining Frobenius series is exactly the predicted q=t^{-1} expression, with omega accounting for the sign twist.

proof_techniques.name: Apolar duality and diagonal harmonics
proof_techniques.description: The paper systematically replaces the quotient R_n=Q[X,Y]/I by the orthogonal complement H_n=I^perp under the apolar form. Multiplication by a variable is adjoint to partial differentiation, so ideals correspond to derivative-stable spaces of harmonic polynomials. This converts statements about quotient classes into statements about polynomial solutions of invariant differential equations p(partial_X,partial_Y)h=0.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For Schur coefficients in the multigraded Hilbert series, one can try to identify the relevant GL_m highest-weight spaces inside the harmonic model. Hook Schur components are often easier to isolate by differential equations and raising/lowering operators than directly in the quotient.

proof_techniques.name: GL_2 and sl_2 weight decomposition
proof_techniques.description: Because the two variable sets form the rows of a 2 by n matrix, GL_2 acts by row operations and commutes with S_n. The sl_2 generators E=sum y_i partial_{x_i}, F=sum x_i partial_{y_i}, and H=sum y_i partial_{y_i}-x_i partial_{x_i} make bidegrees into weight spaces. Finite-dimensional sl_2 representation theory forces the q=t^{-1} specialization and each total-degree diagonal to decompose into symmetric strings.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: The original problem is phrased in terms of Schur polynomials in the grading variables, i.e. GL_m characters. This paper gives the m=2 prototype: Schur coefficients can be attacked by decomposing the multigrading under GL_m, and hook shapes should correspond to explicit highest-weight or exterior-power conditions.

proof_techniques.name: Polarization and straightening using invariant complete symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: The relations h_k(alpha x_k+beta y_k,...,alpha x_n+beta y_n)|_{alpha^r beta^s} in I provide leading terms x_k^r y_k^s with r+s=k. With a lexicographic monomial order, these relations straighten any monomial whose kth variable-pair degree is too large. The method yields spanning monomials, vanishing bounds above total degree binom(n,2), tail-degree vanishing, and one-dimensionality in top total degree.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook Schur coefficients, especially if a combinatorial interpretation comes from monomial or lattice-path data, analogous straightening relations may identify allowed exponent arrays and force finite combinatorial models.

proof_techniques.name: Parking-function model for one-variable Frobenius specialization
proof_techniques.description: The paper relates the conjectural specialization F_n(1,q) to the S_n-module on parking functions, with grading by weight w(f)=n(n+1)/2-sum f(i). Orbits of parking functions correspond to partitions mu contained in the staircase delta_n, and this gives the elementary-symmetric expansion sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur coefficients in a stable GL_m Hilbert series may refine or extend parking-function statistics. This paper identifies the parking-function/staircase-partition data that any hook interpretation should specialize to in small numbers of variable sets.

proof_techniques.name: q-Lagrange inversion as a structural recurrence
proof_techniques.description: The Andrews-Gessel-Garsia q-Lagrange inverse relation produces coefficients h_n^*(q) satisfying a recurrence that matches a decomposition of partitions inside the staircase. Haiman shows explicitly that h_n^*(q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}, and explains how specializations recover parking-function weights, tree inversion enumerators, and q-Catalan numbers.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If hook coefficients satisfy a recursive decomposition by the first column or leg of a hook, the q-Lagrange recurrence gives a template for converting algebraic identities into combinatorial objects such as staircase partitions, trees, or parking functions.

proof_techniques.name: Koszul resolution for character-valued Hilbert series
proof_techniques.description: Several formulas are obtained by resolving a quotient by a homogeneous system of parameters with the Koszul complex. The alternating sum of the graded S_n-characters of the free terms is computed via internal products of Frobenius characteristics, yielding principal Schur-specialization quotients and proving positivity when the quotient exists.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For m-set diagonal coinvariants, hook components may be expressible through quotients by parameter sequences carrying simple GL_m or S_n representations. Koszul computations can turn such presentations into explicit Schur-coefficient formulas.

proof_techniques.name: Operator generation from the Vandermonde
proof_techniques.description: The paper proposes that all diagonal harmonics are generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum y_i partial_{x_i}^p and ordinary derivatives, and proves that allowing a much larger natural Lie algebra of invariant differential operators does not enlarge the generated space. The adjoint formulation converts operator-generated spaces into substitution criteria f(X,phi_a(X)) in the classical coinvariant ideal.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook components often arise by applying a controlled number of variable-set raising operators. The operator-generation viewpoint suggests constructing hook-shaped GL_m highest-weight vectors from Vandermonde derivatives and proving their span by adjoint substitution tests.

proof_techniques.name: Top-degree and low-Y-degree isolation
proof_techniques.description: The top total degree is shown to be extremely small: every bidegree on total degree binom(n,2) is one-dimensional. Separately, the whole component linear in Y is computed using operators E and D and known q-Kostka modules. This isolates a thin slice of the diagonal harmonics where exact Frobenius formulas are accessible.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur shapes include cases with a small exterior leg, which can correspond to being linear or low-degree in some auxiliary variable sets. The proof of Theorem 3.3.1 is a model for deriving exact hook or near-hook components from a small number of operator-generated alternating polynomials.

proof_techniques.name: Exact-sequence interpretation of q-Lagrange identities
proof_techniques.description: Haiman rewrites the q-Lagrange recurrence as an alternating sum of Frobenius series of explicit induced modules and observes that an exact sequence with these terms would prove the parking-function Frobenius conjecture. The terms combine smaller diagonal coinvariant rings with truncated polynomial rings and induction from Young subgroups.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: A hook-coefficient formula might be proved by building a complex whose terms have known Schur expansions and whose Euler characteristic is the desired hook component. The induced-module construction gives a concrete pattern for such complexes.

other_useful_info: Important conjectural content formulated in the paper, not proved there: Conjecture 2.1.1 states dim R_n=(n+1)^{n-1}. Conjecture 2.2.2 states q^{binom(n,2)} H_n(q^{-1},q)=(1+q+...+q^n)^{n-1}. Conjecture 2.3.1 states H_n(1,q) is the inversion enumerator of labelled trees on {0,1,...,n}. Conjectures 2.4.1-2.4.2 identify the ungraded S_n-module R_n with the sign twist of the permutation module on Z_{n+1}^n/Z_{n+1}, equivalently F_n(1,1)=sum_{lambda partition of n} (1/(n+1)) multinomial(n+1; m_0(lambda),...,m_n(lambda)) e_lambda = sum_{lambda partition of n} s_{lambda'}(1^{n+1})/(n+1) s_lambda. Conjecture 2.5.1 gives the q=t^{-1} Frobenius specialization q^{binom(n,2)}F_n(q^{-1},q)=sum_{lambda partition of n} s_{lambda'}(1,q,...,q^n)/(1+q+...+q^n) s_lambda. Conjectures 2.6.3-2.6.4 identify R_n graded by X-degree with the sign twist of the parking-function module graded by weight, equivalently F_n(1,q)=sum_{mu subset delta_n} q^{binom(n,2)-|mu|} e_{lambda(mu)}. Section 2.7 defines the t,q-Catalan polynomial C_n(t,q)=<s_{1^n},F_n(t,q)> as the sign-isotypic multiplicity and records specializations to the Carlitz-Riordan q-Catalan numbers and the q-binomial Catalan 1/[n+1]_q [2n choose n]_q. Section 2.8 reports computational evidence for three variable sets: for n≤5, dim R_n(X,Y,Z)=2n(n+1)^{n-2}, and the sign multiplicity is ((3n+3)(3n+4)...(4n+1))/(3·4·...·(n+1)); it also lists dimensions for four sets for n≤4. Conjecture 5.1.1, the operator conjecture, says H_n is generated from Delta(X) by E_p=sum_i y_i partial_{x_i}^p and partial_{x_i}; it is verified by computer for n≤6 and proved in the paper only in degrees (-,0) and (-,1). Consequences proposed in Section 5.4 include that the socle of R_n consists exactly of alternating polynomials and that a monomial lies in I iff for some k more than n-k indices have combined x_i,y_i exponent at least k; the special case product_i (alpha_i x_i+beta_i y_i) in I is stated to have been proved by Alfano. Related references emphasized by the paper include Andrews, Gessel, and Garsia for q-Lagrange inversion; Kreweras for parking-function/tree bijections; Garsia-Procesi and Bergeron-Garsia for q-Kostka harmonic modules; and Weyl for generators of multisymmetric invariants.
why_relevant: Introduces the diagonal coinvariant ring for the symmetric group and formulates the basic Hilbert-series and representation-theoretic conjectures. It is the foundational source for the algebraic object R_n and its diagonal-invariant quotient construction.
